questions.question: Explain the concept of autoboxing and unboxing in Java with an example code snippet.
keyPoints: Autoboxing: automatic primitive to wrapper conversion
Unboxing: automatic wrapper to primitive conversion

questions.question: What are the eight primitive types and their corresponding wrapper classes in Java?
Explain the Integer caching mechanism in Java and its implications for the == operator.
keyPoints: Java caches Integer values from -128 to 127
Cached values share same object reference
== operator compares references, returns true for cached values
Using new Integer() always creates new object
Values outside cache range return false for ==

questions.question: What is the difference between Integer.parseInt() and Integer.valueOf()?
keyPoints: Both parse String to numeric value
valueOf() uses parseInt() internally then boxes result
Use parseInt() for primitive, valueOf() for wrapper

questions.question: Explain a NullPointerException scenario related to autoboxing with an example.
keyPoints: Example: Integer i = null; int x = i;
Can occur in comparisons, arithmetic, method parameters
Always null-check before unboxing in critical code

questions.question: How does autoboxing work with Java Collections? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: ArrayList<Integer> can store int values directly
list.get(0) returns Integer, unboxed when used
equals compares values, returns true for same numeric value
Always use equals() for value comparison

questions.question: Write a Java program to demonstrate autoboxing and unboxing in method overloading. Explain the method resolution behavior when both primitive and wrapper versions exist.
keyPoints: Create overloaded methods with int and Integer parameters
